architectural_decision.decision: Break the locked v2 curriculum and rebuild around the Report 1 phase-based zero-to-software-engineer path.
architectural_decision.v2_policy: curriculum.v2.json is frozen legacy import data, not the active source of truth.
architectural_decision.core_rule: Keep good lessons, break the old path, optimize for learner progression, proof, documentation quality, and job readiness.
architectural_decision.full_bundle_policy: Split files are authoritative. curriculum.v3.full.generated.json is an optional generated snapshot for review/export only.

assessments.title: Module 00 Assessment — Orientation
assessments.purpose: Verify that the learner can independently apply and explain navigating the course, running exercises, understanding zero magic, and using the architecture tools.

criteria.title: Conceptual clarity
criteria.description: Learner can explain the concepts without memorized magic.

levels.description: Cannot explain course orientation, zero magic philosophy, exercise workflow, architecture overview.
Can explain basics of course orientation, zero magic philosophy, exercise workflow, architecture overview with some gaps.
Clearly explains course orientation, zero magic philosophy, exercise workflow, architecture overview, including mechanics, purpose, and common mistakes.

criteria.title: Implementation
criteria.description: Learner can produce working code or an appropriate artifact.

levels.description: No working implementation; missing or non-functional code.
Partial implementation with minor bugs or gaps.
Complete, clean implementation with proper Go idioms and error handling.

criteria.title: Proof
criteria.description: Learner can verify the work through tests, checks, or review.

levels.description: No proof of understanding; no artifact provided.
Some proof provided but lacks depth or edge cases.
Strong proof with tests or verification covering course orientation, zero magic philosophy and edge cases.

criteria.title: Communication
criteria.description: Learner can communicate decisions and tradeoffs.

levels.description: No explanation provided; unable to discuss the work.
Basic explanation with some clarity but lacks depth.
Professional explanation linking course orientation, zero magic philosophy, exercise workflow, architecture overview to broader system design and trade-offs.

evidence_required: Written reflection on zero magic philosophy
manual_review_questions: Walk me through why a zero-magic learning contract matters and what happens to learners who skip the orientation.
What is the difference between a scaffolded README and a golden README? Why does it matter?
How does the proof-of-understanding system work, and why does it matter more than memorized answers?
assessments.retake_policy: Learner may retry after fixing rubric gaps and writing a short correction note.

assessments.title: Module 01 Assessment — Computers, Terminal, Git, and the Web
assessments.purpose: Verify that the learner can independently apply and explain computer fundamentals, terminal commands, Git version control branching and committing.

criteria.title: Conceptual clarity
criteria.description: Learner can explain the concepts without memorized magic.

levels.description: Cannot explain binary and data representation, terminal and shell, Git workflow, HTTP and web basics.
Can explain basics of binary and data representation, terminal and shell, Git workflow, HTTP and web basics with some gaps.
Clearly explains binary and data representation, terminal and shell, Git workflow, HTTP and web basics, including mechanics, purpose, and common mistakes.

criteria.title: Implementation
criteria.description: Learner can produce working code or an appropriate artifact.

levels.description: No working implementation; missing or non-functional code.
Partial implementation with minor bugs or gaps.
Complete, clean implementation with proper Go idioms and error handling.

criteria.title: Proof
criteria.description: Learner can verify the work through tests, checks, or review.

levels.description: No proof of understanding; no artifact provided.
Some proof provided but lacks depth or edge cases.
Strong proof with tests or verification covering binary and data representation, terminal and shell and edge cases.

criteria.title: Communication
criteria.description: Learner can communicate decisions and tradeoffs.

levels.description: No explanation provided; unable to discuss the work.
Basic explanation with some clarity but lacks depth.
Professional explanation linking binary and data representation, terminal and shell, Git workflow, HTTP and web basics to broader system design and trade-offs.

manual_review_questions: Explain what happens at the OS level when a shell command forks a child process. What is stdin/stdout/stderr?
Describe the Git object model: blobs, trees, commits. How does a branch differ from a tag?
Explain how DNS resolution and TCP work at a high level. What happens between you typing a URL and bytes arriving?
assessments.retake_policy: Learner may retry after fixing rubric gaps and writing a short correction note.

manual_review_questions: What does `go build` actually do? Explain the compilation pipeline from source to binary.
Why does Go enforce import cycles are forbidden? What problem does the module cache solve?
Show me a compiler error you've seen. Explain exactly why the compiler rejected the code.

manual_review_questions: Explain what a slice header contains and why appending to a slice can cause surprising aliasing bugs.
Walk me through the difference between value and pointer receivers. When would you choose each and why?
What is escape analysis? Give me a concrete example of a value that escapes to the heap and one that doesn't.
